waypoints.name: Zeta Tauri
waypoints.instruction: Start at Zeta Tauri, the southern horn tip of Taurus, a 3rd-magnitude star found ENE of Aldebaran

waypoints.name: M1
waypoints.instruction: Move 1 degree NW. M1 is a faint oval smudge about 1 deg N and 1 deg W of Zeta Tauri

routes.source: Turn Left at Orion, Cloudy Nights
routes.notes: Very easy single hop. M1 fits in the same low-power FOV as Zeta Tauri. Mag 8.4, visible in 7x50 binoculars in dark sky. 114 Tau and 121 Tau form a triangle surrounding M1 for confirmation.

waypoints.name: Beta Aquarii (Sadalsuud)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sadalsuud (Beta Aquarii), a 3rd-magnitude star in the western part of Aquarius

waypoints.name: M2
waypoints.instruction: Move 5 degrees due north of Sadalsuud. M2 forms a right triangle with Sadalsuud and Alpha Aquarii (Sadalmelik)

routes.notes: A chain of 4-5 dim stars NE of M2 visible in binoculars/finderscope points toward the cluster. Appears as a compact fuzzy patch in 7x50 binoculars.

waypoints.name: Arcturus
waypoints.instruction: Start at Arcturus, the brightest star in Bootes, easily found by following the arc of the Big Dipper's handle

waypoints.name: Cor Caroli
waypoints.instruction: Identify Cor Caroli (Alpha CVn) about 22 degrees NW of Arcturus

waypoints.name: M3
waypoints.instruction: M3 lies almost exactly halfway along the line from Arcturus to Cor Caroli, slightly closer to Arcturus. Sweep slowly from Arcturus toward Cor Caroli until a fuzzy star appears

routes.notes: One of the most reliable star hops. Alternative: form an isosceles triangle with Arcturus and Rho Bootis. Best seen March through May.

waypoints.name: Antares
waypoints.instruction: Start at Antares, the bright red 1st-magnitude star at the heart of Scorpius

waypoints.name: M4
waypoints.instruction: Move just 1.3 degrees west of Antares. Both fit in the same wide-field eyepiece view. Move Antares out of your FOV to reduce glare

routes.source: EarthSky, Turn Left at Orion, lovethenightsky.com
routes.notes: One of the easiest Messier star hops. Only 1.3 degrees from Antares. Critical to move Antares out of the FOV to see detail in M4. Best in summer months.

waypoints.name: 109 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Start at 109 Virginis, a mag 3.7 star in eastern Virgo

waypoints.name: 110 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Move to 110 Virginis, about 2 degrees east

waypoints.name: 5 Serpentis
waypoints.instruction: Continue in the same direction for about twice the 109-to-110 distance to reach 5 Serpentis

waypoints.name: M5
waypoints.instruction: M5 is just 1/3 degree NW of 5 Serpentis

routes.notes: Alternative: M5 sits at the western apex of a triangle formed with Alpha Serpentis and Mu Serpentis. Distance from 109 Virginis to M5 spans about 8 degrees. Visible from May through July.

waypoints.name: Shaula
waypoints.instruction: Start at Shaula (Lambda Scorpii), the brighter of the two stinger stars at the tip of Scorpius's tail

waypoints.name: M6
waypoints.instruction: Move 5 degrees north-northeast of Shaula. M6 is about three finger-widths NE at arm's length

routes.notes: Alternatively, find M7 first (4.75 deg NE of Shaula along the Lesath-Shaula line) then hop 3 degrees north to M6. The butterfly shape is unmistakable in binoculars. Nearly the apparent size of the full Moon.

waypoints.name: Lesath (Upsilon Scorpii)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Lesath and Shaula, the Cat's Eyes or stinger of Scorpius, at the tip of the scorpion's tail

waypoints.name: Shaula (Lambda Scorpii)
waypoints.instruction: Shaula is the brighter of the stinger pair

waypoints.name: M7
waypoints.instruction: Draw a line from Lesath through Shaula and continue 4.75 degrees NE. M7 is the large obvious star cluster along this line

routes.notes: M7 is visible to the unaided eye on dark moonless nights. Best seen in binoculars due to its large angular size. M6 is 3 degrees north for a nice pair.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii), the star at the top of the Teapot's lid in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: Gamma Sagittarii (Alnasl)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Alnasl (Gamma Sgr), the tip of the Teapot's spout, to the west

waypoints.name: M8
waypoints.instruction: Look about 6 degrees north of the spout, roughly above the line from Alnasl to Kaus Borealis. M8 appears as the bright hazy puff of steam above the teapot's spout

routes.notes: One of the brightest nebulae in the sky. Visible to the unaided eye as a faint patch in dark sky. Use the spout of the Teapot as a pointer - go about twice the spout's distance upward.

waypoints.name: Sabik (Eta Ophiuchi)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sabik (Eta Ophiuchi), a bright mag 2.4 star in the southeastern part of Ophiuchus

waypoints.name: M9
waypoints.instruction: Move 3 degrees SE of Sabik. M9 appears as a small, round, faint nebula in binoculars and small telescopes

routes.notes: Requires dark sky due to its compact size and the rich star field in this region. 26 Ophiuchi makes a good finderscope confirmation star at the 2 o'clock position from M9. Best mid-summer.

waypoints.name: Rasalhague (Alpha Ophiuchi)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Rasalhague, the brightest star in Ophiuchus, at the serpent-bearer's head

waypoints.name: Zeta Ophiuchi
waypoints.instruction: Identify Zeta Ophiuchi to the south of Rasalhague

waypoints.name: 30 Ophiuchi
waypoints.instruction: Find 30 Ophiuchi (mag 4.8), the third star along the SE side of the Rasalhague-Zeta line heading from Rasalhague

waypoints.name: M10
waypoints.instruction: M10 is just 1 degree west of 30 Ophiuchi, visible in the same low-magnification eyepiece view

routes.notes: M12 is 3 degrees NW of M10 for a nice pair. Best viewed May through July.

waypoints.name: Altair
waypoints.instruction: Start at Altair, the brightest star in Aquila and the second brightest in the Summer Triangle

waypoints.name: Lambda Aquilae
waypoints.instruction: Follow the Eagle's body south to Lambda Aquilae (mag 3.4) in your finderscope

waypoints.name: 12 Aquilae
waypoints.instruction: Move SW to the fainter star 12 Aquilae

waypoints.name: M11
waypoints.instruction: Continue about 2 degrees west from 12 Aquilae. M11 appears as a diamond-shaped patch in binoculars

routes.notes: The brighter members form a V-shape resembling a flock of ducks, hence the name. One of the richest open clusters. Best viewed June through September.

waypoints.name: Yed Prior (Delta Ophiuchi)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Yed Prior (mag 2.7) and its close neighbor Yed Posterior (Epsilon Ophiuchi, mag 3.2) in western Ophiuchus

waypoints.name: M12
waypoints.instruction: Move about 8 degrees NE from Yed Prior. Alternatively, M12 is roughly 3 degrees east of Yed Prior

routes.notes: If you have already found M10, M12 is 3 degrees NW of M10. Best viewed May through July.

waypoints.name: Keystone asterism
waypoints.instruction: Locate the Keystone of Hercules, a trapezoidal asterism about 1/3 of the way from Vega to Arcturus. The four stars are Eta, Zeta, Epsilon, and Pi Herculis

waypoints.name: Eta Herculis
waypoints.instruction: Identify Eta Herculis, on the Arcturus-facing (western) side of the Keystone

waypoints.name: M13
waypoints.instruction: M13 lies on the western side of the Keystone, about 2/3 of the way from Eta to Zeta Herculis (2.5 degrees south of Eta)

routes.source: EarthSky, Turn Left at Orion, astrobackyard.com
routes.notes: The finest globular cluster in northern skies. Barely visible to the unaided eye in dark sky. Easily resolved in an 8-inch telescope. Best viewed April through September.

waypoints.name: Cebalrai (Beta Ophiuchi)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Cebalrai (mag 2.8), a prominent star in Ophiuchus

waypoints.name: Sabik (Eta Ophiuchi)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Sabik (Eta Ophiuchi) to the SE

waypoints.name: M14
waypoints.instruction: M14 lies about 1/3 of the way from Cebalrai to Sabik, approximately 8 degrees south and slightly west of Cebalrai

routes.notes: Trickier than most due to the lack of bright stars nearby. If you know M10, look 0.8 degrees north and 10 degrees east. Best viewed May through July. Visible in magnifying finderscopes and binoculars.

waypoints.name: Great Square of Pegasus
waypoints.instruction: Locate the Great Square of Pegasus, then hop along the two bright stars extending westward toward Aquarius

waypoints.name: Enif (Epsilon Pegasi)
waypoints.instruction: Arrive at Enif (Epsilon Pegasi), the brightest star in Pegasus (mag 2.4), at the horse's nose

waypoints.name: M15
waypoints.instruction: Move 4 degrees WNW from Enif. In most binoculars (>4 degree FOV) both Enif and M15 fit in the same field

routes.notes: M15 is mag 6.2, easily visible in small binoculars as a fuzzy out-of-focus star. One of the densest known globular clusters. Best viewed September through November.

waypoints.name: Altair
waypoints.instruction: Start at Altair in Aquila, one of the Summer Triangle stars

waypoints.name: Lambda Aquilae
waypoints.instruction: Follow the Eagle's back southward to Lambda Aquilae

waypoints.name: Gamma Scuti
waypoints.instruction: Continue to Gamma Scuti (mag 4.7) in the constellation Scutum

waypoints.name: M16
waypoints.instruction: M16 is about 2.5 degrees SW of Gamma Scuti. Look for a faint haze surrounding a small star cluster

routes.notes: M16 and M17 are close together and fit in the same binocular field. The famous Pillars of Creation require at least a 12-inch telescope. Alternatively, hop north from M24 through M18 and M17 to reach M16. Best viewed in summer.

waypoints.name: Gamma Scuti
waypoints.instruction: Locate Gamma Scuti (mag 4.7) in the constellation Scutum, found by star-hopping south from Altair through Lambda Aquilae

waypoints.name: M17
waypoints.instruction: M17 lies about 2 degrees SW of Gamma Scuti, slightly south and east of M16. The bright nebula is obvious in binoculars

routes.notes: Alternative route: from M24 (Sagittarius Star Cloud), hop 1 degree north to M18, then 1 more degree north to M17. The swan or checkmark shape is visible in small telescopes. Best viewed in summer.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii), the top star of the Teapot's lid

waypoints.name: M24 (Sagittarius Star Cloud)
waypoints.instruction: Move 7 degrees north and slightly west to locate the large Sagittarius Star Cloud (M24)

waypoints.name: M18
waypoints.instruction: M18 lies about 1 degree north of M24's northern edge, between M24 and M17

routes.notes: Small and often overlooked, sandwiched between the much more impressive M17 and M24. Easiest found by locating M17 and aiming slightly south, or M24 and aiming 2 degrees north. Best June through August.

waypoints.name: Antares
waypoints.instruction: Start at Antares, the bright red heart of Scorpius

waypoints.name: 36 Ophiuchi
waypoints.instruction: Move east from Antares into the Ophiuchus border region

waypoints.name: M19
waypoints.instruction: M19 lies about 8 degrees east of Antares, less than a fist-width. Look for the star that won't quite come to focus near 26 Ophiuchi

routes.notes: 26 Ophiuchi at the 2 o'clock position makes a great confirmation. One of the most oblate (squashed) globular clusters known. Also 4.5 degrees WSW of Theta Ophiuchi. Best in mid-summer.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis, the tip of the Teapot's lid in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M8 (Lagoon Nebula)
waypoints.instruction: Hop NW from the spout of the Teapot to find the Lagoon Nebula (M8) first, the large bright nebula above the spout

waypoints.name: M20
waypoints.instruction: M20 lies about 2 degrees NW of the Lagoon Nebula. In the same binocular field, look for the smaller, fainter fuzzy patch above M8

routes.source: EarthSky, Turn Left at Orion, astrobackyard.com
routes.notes: Best strategy is to find M8 first (easy naked-eye target) then nudge NW to M20. The three-lobed structure that gives the Trifid its name requires a larger telescope to see. Best June through August.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis, the top of the Teapot in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M8 (Lagoon Nebula)
waypoints.instruction: Find M8 above the Teapot's spout

waypoints.name: M20 (Trifid Nebula)
waypoints.instruction: Move 2 degrees NW of M8 to find M20

waypoints.name: M21
waypoints.instruction: M21 is just 0.75 degrees NE of the Trifid Nebula (M20). Both fit in the same low-power telescope FOV

routes.source: Turn Left at Orion (Cambridge), freestarcharts.com, messier-objects.com
routes.notes: Best found in combination with M8 and M20 as a trio. Mag 6.5, visible in smallest binoculars on a dark night. Best June through August.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis, the top of the Teapot's lid in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M22
waypoints.instruction: Move just 2.5 degrees NE of Kaus Borealis. In binoculars centered on Lambda, M22 appears in the 10 o'clock region of the FOV

routes.notes: One of the closest and brightest globulars. At 5th magnitude, visible to the unaided eye under dark skies. In a finderscope, hop from Lambda NE to 24 Sagittarii and M22 is nearby. Best viewed in summer.

waypoints.name: Mu Sagittarii (Polis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Mu Sagittarii, the bright blue giant star above the Teapot asterism in northern Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M23
waypoints.instruction: Move about 1.5 degrees west and a finger's width north of Mu Sagittarii. M23 appears as a hazy patch in binoculars

routes.source: Turn Left at Orion (Cambridge), galactic-hunter.com, freestarcharts.com
routes.notes: Alternative: draw a mental line between Kaus Borealis and Xi Serpentis - M23 is roughly halfway along that line. Apparent size 27 arcminutes. Best viewed June through August.

waypoints.name: Mu Sagittarii (Polis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Mu Sagittarii (Polis), the bright star above the Teapot in northern Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M24
waypoints.instruction: Move 4 degrees north of Mu Sagittarii. M24 is a large, dense Milky Way star cloud easily visible to the naked eye in dark skies

routes.notes: The densest concentration of individual stars visible in binoculars. Under dark skies, easily seen with the unaided eye as a bright patch in the Milky Way. Also about 7 degrees north and slightly west of Kaus Borealis. Best in summer.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis, the top of the Teapot in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M25
waypoints.instruction: Move 6.5 degrees north and slightly east of Kaus Borealis. M25 is 3.5 degrees west of M24 (Sagittarius Star Cloud)

routes.notes: Alternative: 2 degrees north and 4 degrees east from Mu Sagittarii, or 3 degrees south and 2 degrees east of M17. Best viewed in summer.

waypoints.name: Altair
waypoints.instruction: Start at Altair in Aquila

waypoints.name: Delta Aquilae
waypoints.instruction: Move SW to Delta Aquilae

waypoints.name: Lambda Aquilae / 12 Aquilae
waypoints.instruction: Continue curving south to the 4th-magnitude pair Lambda Aquilae and 12 Aquilae

waypoints.name: M11 (Wild Duck Cluster)
waypoints.instruction: Locate M11, the bright Wild Duck Cluster, about 2 degrees west of 12 Aquilae

waypoints.name: M26
waypoints.instruction: Move 3.5 degrees south of M11 to find M26, a smaller and fainter open cluster

routes.notes: M26 is much fainter and more compact than nearby M11. Through an 80mm telescope it appears as a tight misty cluster about half the diameter of the full Moon. Best observed summer months.

waypoints.name: Altair
waypoints.instruction: Start at Altair (Alpha Aquilae), a bright Summer Triangle star

waypoints.name: Sagitta (the Arrow)
waypoints.instruction: Move about 10 degrees north of Altair to find the small constellation Sagitta, a distinctive line of 4 stars forming an arrow

waypoints.name: Gamma Sagittae
waypoints.instruction: Identify Gamma Sagittae, the brightest star in Sagitta, at the arrow's tip pointing east

waypoints.name: M27
waypoints.instruction: Move 3.5 degrees straight north from Gamma Sagittae. M27 appears as a rounded fuzzy patch about 1/4 the size of the full Moon

routes.notes: The largest and brightest planetary nebula in the sky. Bright enough for binoculars. Alternative: rotate Sagitta's shaft line 120 degrees counterclockwise about Gamma Sagittae - where Delta would end up marks M27. Best viewed July through October.

waypoints.name: Kaus Borealis (Lambda Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Borealis, the top of the Teapot's lid in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M28
waypoints.instruction: M28 is less than 1 degree NW of Kaus Borealis. In binoculars centered on Lambda, look for a small faded grey circular area at the 1 o'clock position

routes.notes: Very easy to locate but faint. Invisible to the naked eye. In a finderscope, center on Lambda and shift NW slowly. Often overlooked in favor of the nearby and much brighter M22.

waypoints.name: Sadr (Gamma Cygni)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sadr (Gamma Cygni, mag 2.2), the star at the center of the Northern Cross in Cygnus, where the cross-beam meets the shaft

waypoints.name: M29
waypoints.instruction: Move just 1.7 degrees south and slightly west of Sadr. M29 appears as a tight knot of stars

routes.notes: Very easy to locate from Sadr but unimpressive - a small sparse cluster sometimes called a mini-Pleiades. Visible in 10x50 binoculars. Best viewed July through October.

waypoints.name: Zeta Capricorni
waypoints.instruction: Start at Zeta Capricorni (mag 3.8), in the lower-left part of Capricornus's smile-shaped constellation pattern

waypoints.name: M30
waypoints.instruction: Move 3.3 degrees ESE of Zeta Capricorni. The mag 5.2 star 41 Capricorni lies just east of the cluster and helps confirm your position

routes.notes: One of the more challenging Messier objects to find due to the faintness of Capricornus. The constellation never rises high above the southern horizon for northern observers. Best viewed September through October.

waypoints.name: Alpheratz
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alpheratz (Alpha And), the NE corner of the Great Square of Pegasus

waypoints.name: Delta Andromedae
waypoints.instruction: Hop about 7 degrees NE along the Andromeda chain to Delta Andromedae (mag +3.3)

waypoints.name: Mirach
waypoints.instruction: Continue another 8 degrees NE to Mirach (Beta And, mag +2.1)

waypoints.name: Mu Andromedae
waypoints.instruction: From Mirach, turn NW about 4 degrees to Mu Andromedae (mag +3.9)

waypoints.name: M31
waypoints.instruction: M31 is about 1 degree W of Mu And, visible as an elongated fuzzy glow

routes.source: Standard Andromeda chain route used by nearly all observing guides
routes.notes: The classic Alpheratz-Mirach-Mu And route. M31 is visible to the naked eye under dark skies.

waypoints.name: Alpheratz
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alpheratz, NE corner of the Great Square of Pegasus

waypoints.name: Mirach
waypoints.instruction: Hop NE along the Andromeda chain to Mirach (Beta And)

waypoints.name: Mu Andromedae
waypoints.instruction: From Mirach, turn NW about 4 degrees to Mu Andromedae

waypoints.name: M31
waypoints.instruction: Find M31 about 1 degree W of Mu And

waypoints.name: M32
waypoints.instruction: M32 is 22 arcmin S of M31's core - look for a small, bright fuzzy star just S of the galaxy's disk

routes.source: Standard route - M32 is found after locating M31
routes.notes: M32 appears as a bright compact companion south of M31's nucleus. Visible in 8x40 binoculars.

waypoints.name: Mirach
waypoints.instruction: Start at Mirach (Beta And, mag +2.1) in the Andromeda chain

waypoints.name: M33
waypoints.instruction: From Mirach, M33 lies about 7 degrees to the SW, roughly equidistant from Mirach as M31 is but in the opposite direction. A line from M31 through Mirach extended the same distance lands near M33.

routes.source: Mirach bisector method from multiple observing guides
routes.notes: M33 is a large, low-surface-brightness galaxy. Easier in binoculars than telescopes due to wide field needed. Can be difficult even when pointing right at it.

waypoints.name: Algol
waypoints.instruction: Start at Algol (Beta Persei, mag +2.1), the famous eclipsing variable 'Demon Star'

waypoints.name: M34
waypoints.instruction: M34 lies about 5 degrees NW of Algol, roughly halfway along a line from Algol to Almach (Gamma And). Look for a scattered cluster with a distinctive X-shaped pattern of bright stars.

routes.source: Algol-Almach midpoint method from freestarcharts and Astronomy Now
routes.notes: Easy binocular target about 2 binocular fields NW of Algol. The bright stars form a recognizable X pattern.

waypoints.name: Castor
waypoints.instruction: Identify Castor and Pollux, the twin bright stars of Gemini

waypoints.name: Tejat (Mu Gem)
waypoints.instruction: From Castor, look SW about 10 degrees to find Tejat (Mu Gem, mag +2.9) at Castor's foot

waypoints.name: Propus (Eta Gem)
waypoints.instruction: Continue about 2 degrees W to Propus (Eta Gem, mag +3.3)

waypoints.name: M35
waypoints.instruction: M35 is about 2 degrees NW of Propus. A large, rich cluster visible to the naked eye from dark sites.

routes.source: Standard Gemini foot route from multiple observing guides
routes.notes: M35 lies near Castor's foot. Also findable by drawing a line from Betelgeuse through Alhena and extending it.

waypoints.name: Theta Aurigae
waypoints.instruction: Find Theta Aurigae (mag +2.6), about 8 degrees S of Menkalinan in the Auriga polygon

waypoints.name: El Nath
waypoints.instruction: Note El Nath (Beta Tau, mag +1.7) about 11 degrees SW of Theta Aur - these two stars bracket the cluster trio

waypoints.name: M36
waypoints.instruction: M36 lies just W of the midpoint of the line connecting Theta Aur and El Nath

routes.source: Theta Aur / El Nath midpoint method from nightskyinfo.com and freestarcharts
routes.notes: M36, M37, and M38 all lie along the Theta Aur - El Nath line. All three fit in a wide binocular field.

waypoints.name: Theta Aurigae
waypoints.instruction: Start at Theta Aurigae (mag +2.6)

waypoints.name: El Nath
waypoints.instruction: Note El Nath (Beta Tau, mag +1.7) about 11 degrees SW

waypoints.name: M36
waypoints.instruction: Find M36 just W of the midpoint of the Theta Aur - El Nath line

waypoints.name: M37
waypoints.instruction: From M36, continue about 4 degrees SE along the same line to find M37, the richest of the three Auriga clusters

routes.source: Auriga cluster trio route from nightskyinfo.com and Astronomy Now
routes.notes: M37 is the brightest and richest of the three Auriga clusters. It lies just E of the Theta Aur - El Nath midpoint.

waypoints.name: Theta Aurigae
waypoints.instruction: Start at Theta Aurigae (mag +2.6)

waypoints.name: El Nath
waypoints.instruction: Note El Nath (Beta Tau, mag +1.7) about 11 degrees SW

waypoints.name: M36
waypoints.instruction: Find M36 at the midpoint of the Theta Aur - El Nath line

waypoints.name: M38
waypoints.instruction: M38 is about 2.5 degrees NW of M36, appearing as a vaguely cruciform (cross-shaped) cluster

routes.source: Auriga cluster trio route from nightskyinfo.com and freestarcharts
routes.notes: M38 has a characteristic cross or starfish shape. At mag +7.0 it requires binoculars.

waypoints.name: Deneb
waypoints.instruction: Start at Deneb (Alpha Cyg, mag +1.25), the tail of the Swan and NE vertex of the Summer Triangle

waypoints.name: Rho Cygni
waypoints.instruction: Hop about 9 degrees E of Deneb to find Rho Cygni (mag +4.0)

waypoints.name: M39
waypoints.instruction: M39 is about 3 degrees N of Rho Cygni - look for a large, loose triangular cluster of bright stars

routes.source: Deneb to Rho Cygni route from freestarcharts and Astronomy Now
routes.notes: M39 spans over 30 arcmin (larger than the full Moon). Best at low magnification in binoculars.

waypoints.name: Megrez
waypoints.instruction: Start at Megrez (Delta UMa, mag +3.3), the dimmest star in the Big Dipper where the bowl meets the handle

waypoints.name: 70 Ursae Majoris
waypoints.instruction: Hop 1.1 degrees NE to 70 UMa (mag +5.5)

waypoints.name: M40
waypoints.instruction: Continue 0.25 degrees in the same direction to find M40, a pair of mag +9.6 and +10.1 stars separated by about 50 arcsec

routes.source: Megrez hop from Universe Today and freestarcharts
routes.notes: M40 is simply an optical double star, not a deep-sky object. It was a Messier catalog error but remains listed.

waypoints.name: Sirius
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sirius (Alpha CMa, mag -1.46), the brightest star in the night sky - found by following Orion's belt downward

waypoints.name: M41
waypoints.instruction: Center Sirius and sweep exactly 4 degrees due S. M41 appears as a large, bright, loose cluster visible to the naked eye in dark skies.

routes.source: Direct Sirius hop from EarthSky, freestarcharts, and multiple guides
routes.notes: One of the simplest star hops in the Messier catalog. M41 forms a rough equilateral triangle with Sirius and Nu2 CMa.

waypoints.name: Alnilam
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alnilam, the middle star of Orion's Belt

waypoints.name: Orion's Sword
waypoints.instruction: Look about 3 degrees directly S of the Belt for a short chain of 3 stars forming Orion's Sword

waypoints.name: M42
waypoints.instruction: M42 is the middle 'star' in the Sword - it appears fuzzy to the naked eye. Through any optics you will see bright nebulosity surrounding the Trapezium star cluster.

routes.source: Standard Orion's Sword route from NASA, Cosmos Safari, and all beginner guides
routes.notes: The easiest nebula to find. Visible to the naked eye. The Trapezium (4 stars in a diamond) is at its heart.

waypoints.name: Alnilam
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alnilam, the middle star of Orion's Belt

waypoints.name: M42
waypoints.instruction: Find M42 in the middle of Orion's Sword, 3 degrees S of the Belt

waypoints.name: M43
waypoints.instruction: M43 is just 8 arcmin N of M42's core, separated by a dark dust lane called the Fish's Mouth. It appears as a comma-shaped glow around a central star.

routes.source: M42 companion from freestarcharts and Messier Objects
routes.notes: M43 is physically part of the same nebula complex as M42. It requires a telescope to distinguish from M42.

waypoints.name: Pollux
waypoints.instruction: Identify Pollux (Beta Gem, mag +1.1) and Castor, the twin bright stars of Gemini

waypoints.name: Regulus
waypoints.instruction: Note Regulus (Alpha Leo, mag +1.4) about 30 degrees to the ESE

waypoints.name: M44
waypoints.instruction: M44 lies roughly halfway between the Pollux-Castor pair and Regulus, in the center of the faint Y-shape of Cancer. Look for a fuzzy patch to the naked eye that resolves into a swarm of stars in binoculars.

routes.source: Pollux-Regulus midpoint method from EarthSky and Constellation Guide
routes.notes: M44 is best in binoculars or the naked eye - too large for most telescopes. Visible as a fuzzy patch unaided in dark skies.

waypoints.name: Orion's Belt
waypoints.instruction: Start at the 3 belt stars of Orion and follow the line they form upward (NW)

waypoints.name: Aldebaran
waypoints.instruction: The line leads to Aldebaran, the bright orange eye of Taurus, surrounded by the V-shaped Hyades cluster

waypoints.name: M45
waypoints.instruction: Continue in the same direction about 14 degrees NW past Aldebaran. M45 appears as a compact, brilliant group of blue-white stars visible to the naked eye.

routes.source: Orion's Belt pointer method from EarthSky, StarWalk, and all beginner guides
routes.notes: No optics needed to find M45 - it is one of the most recognizable naked-eye objects. Best in binoculars for the full cluster.

waypoints.name: Sirius
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sirius (Alpha CMa), the brightest star in the night sky

waypoints.name: M47
waypoints.instruction: From Sirius, scan about 12 degrees E (and slightly S). M47 appears first as a bright, loose cluster.

waypoints.name: M46
waypoints.instruction: M46 lies just 1.3 degrees ESE of M47 in the same binocular field. M46 is fainter and more compact, and contains a planetary nebula (NGC 2438) on its northern edge.

routes.source: Sirius eastward scan from Astronomy Now, freestarcharts, and The Astro Gazer
routes.notes: M46 and M47 are a classic pair visible together in binoculars. The planetary nebula in M46 is a bonus in telescopes.

waypoints.name: Sirius
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sirius (Alpha CMa), the brightest star in the night sky

waypoints.name: Mirzam
waypoints.instruction: Note Mirzam (Beta CMa, mag +2.0) about 5 degrees W of Sirius

waypoints.name: M47
waypoints.instruction: Extend a line from Mirzam through Sirius and continue about 14 degrees NE. M47 is a bright, scattered cluster of about 50 stars.

routes.notes: M47 is the brighter of the Puppis pair. It and M46 are separated by just 1.3 degrees.

waypoints.name: Procyon
waypoints.instruction: Start at Procyon (Alpha CMi, mag +0.4), the bright star in Canis Minor

waypoints.name: Zeta Monocerotis
waypoints.instruction: Head about 14 degrees SE from Procyon. Locate Zeta Monocerotis (mag +4.4) as an intermediate reference.

waypoints.name: M48
waypoints.instruction: M48 lies about 3 degrees SSE of Zeta Monocerotis, near the Hydra-Monoceros border. It is a large cluster spanning nearly a full degree.

routes.source: Zeta Monocerotis hop from freestarcharts and Astronomy.com
routes.notes: M48 covers almost twice the apparent size of the full Moon. Best at low magnification.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Vir, mag +2.8), located between Denebola and Arcturus, shifted toward Spica

waypoints.name: M49
waypoints.instruction: M49 lies about 4.1 degrees WSW of Vindemiatrix. It is the brightest galaxy in the Virgo Cluster (mag +8.4) and appears as a small oval glow.

routes.source: Vindemiatrix offset from freestarcharts and Messier Objects
routes.notes: M49 sits about 5 degrees S of the main Virgo Cluster chain. Requires dark skies. Faintly visible in large binoculars.

waypoints.name: Sirius
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sirius (Alpha CMa), the brightest star in the night sky

waypoints.name: Theta Canis Majoris
waypoints.instruction: Hop about 5 degrees N of Sirius to Theta CMa (mag +4.1)

waypoints.name: M50
waypoints.instruction: Continue the same line another 4-5 degrees N from Theta CMa to reach M50, a heart-shaped cluster at the Monoceros-CMa border

routes.source: Sirius-Theta CMa line from freestarcharts and BBC Sky at Night
routes.notes: M50 is about 9.5 degrees NNE of Sirius total. Through a scope it shows a distinctive heart shape of blue-white stars.

waypoints.name: Alkaid
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alkaid (Eta UMa, mag +1.9), the end star of the Big Dipper's handle

waypoints.name: 24 Canum Venaticorum
waypoints.instruction: Hop about 3 degrees SW to 24 CVn (mag +4.7)

waypoints.name: M51
waypoints.instruction: M51 is about 2 degrees further S, forming a right angle with Alkaid and 24 CVn. In a telescope, look for two fuzzy patches (M51a and its companion NGC 5195) in contact.

routes.source: Alkaid hop from Cloudy Nights, Messier Objects, and Astronomy Now
routes.notes: One of the most popular galaxies. The spiral arms require an 8-inch or larger scope under dark skies.

waypoints.name: Schedar
waypoints.instruction: Start at Schedar (Alpha Cas, mag +2.2), on the right side of Cassiopeia's W

waypoints.name: Caph
waypoints.instruction: Note Caph (Beta Cas, mag +2.3), about 5 degrees to the NW of Schedar

waypoints.name: M52
waypoints.instruction: Draw a line from Schedar through Caph and extend it about 6 degrees in the same direction. M52 is a rich, compact cluster near the reddish star 4 Cas.

routes.source: Schedar-Caph extension from Love the Night Sky, freestarcharts, and Constellation Guide
routes.notes: A rich cluster of about 200 stars. The Schedar-Caph-M52 line is one of the most reliable star hops in the northern sky.

waypoints.name: Arcturus
waypoints.instruction: Start at Arcturus (Alpha Boo, mag -0.05) - follow the arc of the Big Dipper's handle to find it

waypoints.name: Muphrid
waypoints.instruction: Find Muphrid (Eta Boo, mag +2.7) about 3 degrees W of Arcturus

waypoints.name: Diadem
waypoints.instruction: Continue the line from Arcturus through Muphrid about 11 degrees W to Diadem (Alpha Com, mag +4.3)

waypoints.name: M53
waypoints.instruction: M53 is just 1 degree NE of Diadem. Look for a small, round, bright fuzzy patch.

routes.source: Arcturus-Muphrid-Diadem route from Messier Objects and freestarcharts
routes.notes: M53 is one of the more remote Milky Way globulars at 58,000 light-years. Needs a 6-inch scope to begin resolving stars.

waypoints.name: Ascella
waypoints.instruction: Start at Ascella (Zeta Sgr, mag +2.6), the star at the base of the Teapot's handle in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: M54
waypoints.instruction: M54 is just 1.75 degrees WSW of Ascella (0.5 deg S, 1.5 deg W). It appears as a small, unresolved fuzzy spot.

routes.source: Ascella offset from freestarcharts and Messier Objects
routes.notes: M54 is actually a globular cluster of the Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy, not the Milky Way. Cannot be resolved in amateur scopes.

waypoints.name: Ascella
waypoints.instruction: Start at Ascella (Zeta Sgr, mag +2.6) at the base of the Teapot's handle

waypoints.name: Theta1/Theta2 Sgr
waypoints.instruction: About 5 degrees SE of Ascella, find the close pair Theta1 and Theta2 Sagittarii

waypoints.name: M55
waypoints.instruction: From Theta1/Theta2, M55 is about 4 degrees to the NW. Alternatively, about 7 degrees E and slightly S of Ascella. Look for a large, diffuse, resolved globular.

routes.source: Theta Sgr route from freestarcharts and Deep Sky Corner
routes.notes: M55 has low surface brightness and requires dark skies. It is one of the more challenging Messier globulars from northern latitudes due to its low declination (-31 deg).

waypoints.name: Albireo
waypoints.instruction: Start at Albireo (Beta Cyg, mag +3.1), the famous gold-and-blue double star at the head of Cygnus

waypoints.name: Sulafat
waypoints.instruction: Note Sulafat (Gamma Lyr, mag +3.2), the second-brightest star in Lyra, about 7.5 degrees to the WSW

waypoints.name: M56
waypoints.instruction: M56 lies almost halfway along the Albireo-Sulafat line, about 4.5 degrees from Albireo. It appears as a small fuzzy patch.

routes.source: Albireo-Sulafat midpoint from freestarcharts, Messier Objects, and Skyscrapers
routes.notes: M56 is a compact globular that requires an 8-inch scope to begin resolving. The Albireo-Sulafat line is very reliable.

waypoints.name: Vega
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vega (Alpha Lyr, mag +0.0), the brilliant blue-white star and brightest in Lyra

waypoints.name: Sheliak
waypoints.instruction: Sweep about 6 degrees SE from Vega to Sheliak (Beta Lyr, mag +3.5)

waypoints.name: Sulafat
waypoints.instruction: Sulafat (Gamma Lyr, mag +3.2) is about 2 degrees ESE of Sheliak - these two stars form the bottom of Lyra's parallelogram

waypoints.name: M57
waypoints.instruction: M57 lies about 40% of the way from Sheliak to Sulafat. At 100x+ magnification it shows the classic smoke ring shape.

routes.source: Sheliak-Sulafat midpoint from NASA, Astronomy Now, and freestarcharts
routes.notes: The Ring Nebula is tiny (about 1 arcmin) but bright. Use 100x or more to see the ring shape. The central hole is visible in 4-inch scopes.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Vir, mag +2.8)

waypoints.name: Denebola
waypoints.instruction: Note the direction toward Denebola (Beta Leo, mag +2.1), roughly 15 degrees to the WNW

waypoints.name: M60
waypoints.instruction: Head about 4.5 degrees W of Vindemiatrix along the Vindemiatrix-Denebola line to find M60 (brightest of this trio)

waypoints.name: M59
waypoints.instruction: M59 is about 20 arcmin WNW of M60, in the same eyepiece field

waypoints.name: M58
waypoints.instruction: Continue about 1 degree further WNW from M59 to reach M58

routes.source: Vindemiatrix chain from freestarcharts, SEDS Messier, and Astronomy.com
routes.notes: M58, M59, and M60 form a chain best swept in sequence. All are faint galaxies requiring dark skies and patience.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Vir, mag +2.8)

waypoints.name: 34 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Hop 1 degree N and 3.5 degrees W to the double star 34 Vir (mag +6.1)

waypoints.name: M60
waypoints.instruction: About 1 degree SW of 34 Vir lies M60

waypoints.name: M59
waypoints.instruction: M59 is about 20 arcmin WNW of M60, in the same wide-field eyepiece view

routes.source: Vindemiatrix to 34 Vir route from SEDS Messier and Astronomy.com
routes.notes: M59 is about 5 degrees due W of Vindemiatrix. It and M60 are close enough to fit in a single low-power field.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Vir, mag +2.8)

waypoints.name: 34 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Hop 1 degree N and 3.5 degrees W to find the double star 34 Vir (mag +6.1)

waypoints.name: M60
waypoints.instruction: About 1 degree SW of 34 Vir lies M60 (mag +8.8), the brightest galaxy in this trio, with companion NGC 4647 nearby

routes.source: Vindemiatrix to 34 Vir route from SEDS Messier, freestarcharts, and Astronomy.com
routes.notes: M60 is the E-most and brightest of the M58-M59-M60 chain. The nearby NGC 4647 is an interacting companion visible in medium scopes.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8), the bright star marking the eastern arm of Virgo

waypoints.name: 16 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Move about 8 degrees south-southwest to the mag 5.0 star 16 Virginis

waypoints.name: M61
waypoints.instruction: M61 is 1.25 degrees north-northeast of 16 Virginis. Look for a faint, round glow

routes.notes: M61 has low surface brightness; best under dark skies. Also reachable from M49 by moving 3.5 degrees south and 2 degrees west.

waypoints.name: Eta Virginis (Zaniah)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Eta Virginis (mag 3.9), part of the Bowl of Virgo asterism

waypoints.name: 16 Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Move about 5 degrees north to find 16 Virginis (mag 5.0)

waypoints.name: M61
waypoints.instruction: M61 lies 1.25 degrees north-northeast of 16 Virginis

routes.notes: Alternative route if Vindemiatrix is too far; uses the Bowl of Virgo as orientation

waypoints.name: Antares (Alpha Scorpii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Antares (mag 1.1), the red heart of Scorpius

waypoints.name: M62
waypoints.instruction: Move 7.5 degrees southeast of Antares. M62 appears as a small hazy patch in binoculars

routes.notes: M62 sits in rich Milky Way star fields and can be easily missed. An 8-inch telescope is needed to resolve outer stars.

waypoints.name: Epsilon Scorpii (Larawag)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Epsilon Scorpii (mag 2.3), just southeast of Antares in the Scorpion's body

waypoints.name: M62
waypoints.instruction: Move 4.75 degrees northeast to M62

routes.notes: Closer starting point than Antares; M62 is a lopsided globular cluster

waypoints.name: Cor Caroli (Alpha CVn)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Cor Caroli (mag 2.9), the brightest star in Canes Venatici, found one-third of the way from Alkaid to Denebola

waypoints.name: 20 CVn / Gamma-shaped asterism
waypoints.instruction: Place Cor Caroli at the southwestern edge of your binocular field. On the opposite side, find four stars forming a right-triangle pattern

waypoints.name: M63
waypoints.instruction: M63 is about 1 degree north of 19 Canum Venaticorum, the star at the right angle of the triangle. It appears as a faint elongated glow

routes.notes: About 4 degrees northeast of Cor Caroli. Visible as a bright, compact patch in 10x50 binoculars under dark skies.

waypoints.name: Diadem (Alpha Comae Berenices)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Diadem (mag 4.3), the gem in Queen Berenice's crown, located about 15 degrees west of Arcturus

waypoints.name: 35 Comae Berenices
waypoints.instruction: Star hop approximately 4 degrees north-northwest to 35 Comae Berenices (mag 5.1)

waypoints.name: M64
waypoints.instruction: M64 is about 1 degree northeast of 35 Comae Berenices. Look for a bright core with a distinctive dark patch (the 'black eye') visible in 4-inch or larger telescopes

routes.notes: M64 lies 19 degrees west and slightly north of Arcturus. The dark dust band is visible in moderate apertures.

waypoints.name: Chertan (Theta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Chertan (mag 3.3), in the haunches of Leo, forming a right triangle with Zosma and Denebola

waypoints.name: 73 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Move 2 degrees south to find 73 Leonis (mag 5.3)

waypoints.name: M65
waypoints.instruction: M65 lies 0.75 degrees east of 73 Leonis

waypoints.name: M66
waypoints.instruction: M66 is a further 0.33 degrees southeast of M65. Both fit in the same low-power field with NGC 3628 just 0.5 degrees north

routes.notes: The Leo Triplet (M65, M66, NGC 3628) all fit in one field of view. Best in March-May.

waypoints.name: Chertan (Theta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Chertan (mag 3.3) in Leo's haunches

waypoints.name: 73 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Move 2 degrees south to 73 Leonis (mag 5.3)

waypoints.name: M65
waypoints.instruction: Move 0.75 degrees east to M65

waypoints.name: M66
waypoints.instruction: M66 is 0.33 degrees southeast of M65, slightly brighter with more obvious spiral structure

routes.notes: Same hop as M65. M66 is the brightest of the Leo Triplet.

waypoints.name: Acubens (Alpha Cancri)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Acubens (mag 4.2), the star marking the southeastern corner of the Crab's body

waypoints.name: M67
waypoints.instruction: Move about 1.5 degrees west of Acubens. M67 is a rich open cluster visible in binoculars, one of the oldest known open clusters

routes.notes: Easy one-hop target from Alpha Cancri. M67 is 3-5 billion years old, unusually ancient for an open cluster.

waypoints.name: Kraz (Beta Corvi)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kraz (mag 2.6), one of the stars forming the rectangle of Corvus the Crow

waypoints.name: M68
waypoints.instruction: Move 3.5 degrees southeast of Kraz. M68 appears as a faint patch in binoculars; a 4-inch telescope begins to resolve its brightest stars

routes.notes: M68 lies roughly halfway between Beta Corvi and Spica. Contains over 100,000 stars.

waypoints.name: Kaus Australis (Epsilon Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Australis (mag 1.8), the brightest star in the Teapot's base in Sagittarius

waypoints.name: Ascella (Zeta Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Ascella (mag 2.6) at the other end of the Teapot's base, about 4 degrees east

waypoints.name: M69
waypoints.instruction: M69 lies about 2 degrees northeast of Ascella, roughly 1.5 degrees north of the imaginary line connecting Kaus Australis and Ascella

routes.notes: M69 and M70 are close neighbors inside the Teapot. Best viewed in summer from southern latitudes.

waypoints.name: Kaus Australis (Epsilon Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Kaus Australis (mag 1.8) at the base of the Sagittarius Teapot

waypoints.name: Ascella (Zeta Sagittarii)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Ascella (mag 2.6) at the eastern end of the Teapot's base

waypoints.name: M70
waypoints.instruction: M70 is positioned almost exactly halfway along the line connecting Kaus Australis and Ascella, slightly above the line

routes.notes: Nearly identical in size and brightness to neighbor M69, about 2 degrees to its southwest.

waypoints.name: Gamma Sagittae
waypoints.instruction: Start at Gamma Sagittae (mag 3.5), one of the stars forming the Arrow constellation inside the Summer Triangle

waypoints.name: Delta Sagittae
waypoints.instruction: Identify Delta Sagittae (mag 3.7), forming the other end of the arrow shaft

waypoints.name: M71
waypoints.instruction: M71 lies halfway between Gamma and Delta Sagittae, slightly south of the line connecting them. It appears as a hazy patch in binoculars

routes.notes: One of the easiest Messier objects to locate. M71 is a very loose globular cluster at mag 6.1.

waypoints.name: Albali (Epsilon Aquarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Albali (mag 3.8) in the western part of Aquarius

waypoints.name: M72
waypoints.instruction: Move about 3 degrees south and 1.5 degrees east of Albali. M72 is a faint, remote globular cluster needing 8-inch or larger aperture to look good

routes.notes: M72 and M73 are only 1.5 degrees apart. One of the most remote Messier globulars at 53,000 light-years.

waypoints.name: Albali (Epsilon Aquarii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Albali (mag 3.8) in Aquarius

waypoints.name: M72
waypoints.instruction: Locate M72, about 3 degrees south and 1.5 degrees east of Albali

waypoints.name: M73
waypoints.instruction: From M72, move 1.5 degrees east to M73, an asterism of four stars in a Y-shape

routes.notes: M73 is technically an asterism, not a true cluster. Only four stars in a distinctive pattern.

waypoints.name: Eta Piscium (Alpherg)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Eta Piscium (mag 3.6). To find it, extend a line from Hamal through Sheratan in Aries about 7.5 degrees southwest

waypoints.name: M74
waypoints.instruction: M74 is 1.5 degrees east-northeast of Eta Piscium. It is a face-on grand-design spiral with very low surface brightness, one of the hardest Messier objects

routes.notes: Extremely low surface brightness makes M74 one of the most challenging Messier objects. Requires dark skies and averted vision.

waypoints.name: 59 Sagittarii
waypoints.instruction: Locate the asterism of 58 (Omega), 59, 60, and 62 Sagittarii in eastern Sagittarius near the Capricornus border

waypoints.name: M75
waypoints.instruction: The cross-bar formed by 59 and 60 Sagittarii points almost directly NNE to M75, about 5.25 degrees away. Alternatively, M75 lies 2.75 degrees west of 4 Capricorni

routes.notes: One of the more difficult Messier objects to locate, in a sparse star field. A very concentrated globular cluster.

waypoints.name: 51 Andromedae
waypoints.instruction: Start at 51 Andromedae (mag 3.5)

waypoints.name: Phi Persei
waypoints.instruction: Move about 2 degrees north-northeast to Phi Persei (mag 4.0)

waypoints.name: M76
waypoints.instruction: M76 is less than 1 degree north-northwest of Phi Persei. At mag 12.2, it is the faintest Messier object but visible even in a 2.4-inch telescope as an elongated patch

routes.source: Love the Night Sky / Astronomy Now
routes.notes: Faintest object in the Messier catalog. Also findable roughly halfway between Epsilon Cassiopeiae and Mirfak (Alpha Persei).

waypoints.name: Menkar (Alpha Ceti)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Menkar (mag 2.5) in Cetus as an orientation anchor

waypoints.name: Gamma Ceti
waypoints.instruction: Move 5 degrees west from Menkar to Gamma Ceti (mag 3.5)

waypoints.name: Delta Ceti
waypoints.instruction: Move 3 degrees south and slightly west to Delta Ceti (mag 4.1)

waypoints.name: M77
waypoints.instruction: M77 lies about 1 degree southeast of Delta Ceti. It has a bright core visible in binoculars

routes.notes: Brightest and nearest Seyfert galaxy. Easy to spot with binoculars thanks to its compact, bright core.

waypoints.name: Alnitak (Zeta Orionis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alnitak (mag 1.7), the easternmost star of Orion's Belt

waypoints.name: M78
waypoints.instruction: Move about 2 degrees north and 1.5 degrees east of Alnitak. M78 is the brightest diffuse reflection nebula in the sky, appearing like a small faint comet in a 4-inch telescope

routes.notes: Alternative approach: from Mintaka (westernmost belt star), track east at the same declination until M78 enters the field.

waypoints.name: Arneb (Alpha Leporis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Arneb (mag 2.6), the brightest star in Lepus, found by extending a line from Betelgeuse through Saiph

waypoints.name: Nihal (Beta Leporis)
waypoints.instruction: Move 3 degrees southwest to Nihal (mag 2.8)

waypoints.name: HR 1771
waypoints.instruction: Continue the same direction for about 1.5 times the Arneb-Nihal distance to reach the 5th-magnitude star HR 1771

waypoints.name: M79
waypoints.instruction: M79 is only 0.5 degrees northeast of HR 1771, visible in the same binocular field as a single fuzzy star

routes.notes: Unusual winter globular cluster. One of few globulars visible in the winter sky.

waypoints.name: Antares (Alpha Scorpii)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Antares (mag 1.1), the bright red heart of Scorpius

waypoints.name: Graffias (Beta Scorpii)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Graffias (mag 2.5) in the claws of Scorpius, northwest of Antares

waypoints.name: M80
waypoints.instruction: M80 is located almost exactly halfway between Antares and Graffias, about 4.5 degrees northwest of Antares. It appears as a fuzzy ball of light in small telescopes

routes.source: Love the Night Sky / Messier Objects
routes.notes: Very easy to find given the two bright anchor stars. 8-inch telescopes show the bright nucleus and extended halo.

waypoints.name: Phecda (Gamma Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Phecda at the lower-left corner of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: Dubhe (Alpha Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Draw a diagonal line across the bowl from Phecda to Dubhe (mag 1.8), about 10 degrees

waypoints.name: M81
waypoints.instruction: Continue this line the same distance beyond Dubhe (about 10 degrees northwest) into a sparse star field to find M81, a bright oval in binoculars

waypoints.name: M82
waypoints.instruction: M82 (Cigar Galaxy) lies only 38 arcminutes directly north of M81, both fit in one low-power field

routes.notes: One of the most popular star hops for beginners. M81 is mag 6.9 and easy in binoculars. The pair is one of the finest galaxy duos in the sky.

waypoints.name: Phecda (Gamma Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Phecda at the lower-left corner of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: Dubhe (Alpha Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Draw a line from Phecda through Dubhe (the diagonal of the bowl)

waypoints.name: M81
waypoints.instruction: Continue the same distance beyond Dubhe (about 10 degrees) to find M81

waypoints.name: M82
waypoints.instruction: M82 is 38 arcminutes due north of M81. It appears as a thin, elongated streak compared to M81's round glow

routes.notes: Same hop as M81. M82 is an edge-on starburst galaxy with distinctive mottled texture.

waypoints.name: Gamma Hydrae
waypoints.instruction: Start at Gamma Hydrae (mag 3.0), a prominent star in the body of Hydra

waypoints.name: Trail of faint stars
waypoints.instruction: Follow a trail of 5th-7th magnitude stars about 6.5 degrees south and 3 degrees east

waypoints.name: M83
waypoints.instruction: M83 is a bright face-on barred spiral, the finest visible to Northern Hemisphere observers. Spiral structure visible in 6-inch telescopes

routes.notes: Low declination makes it challenging for northern observers. Also reachable from Pi Hydrae (3 degrees south, 6 degrees west).

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1), the tail star of Leo

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8) about 15 degrees east in Virgo

waypoints.name: M84
waypoints.instruction: M84 lies almost exactly halfway between Denebola and Vindemiatrix. It marks the western end of Markarian's Chain, with M86 just 17 arcminutes to the east in the same eyepiece field

routes.source: BBC Sky at Night / Messier Objects
routes.notes: Gateway to Markarian's Chain. M84 and M86 are a famous pair visible simultaneously in the same low-power field.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1), the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: T-shaped asterism
waypoints.instruction: Look 7 degrees east with a finderscope to find a T-shaped asterism of five stars including 6 Virginis

waypoints.name: 11 Comae Berenices
waypoints.instruction: About 2 degrees above the top of the T-shape, find 11 Comae Berenices (mag 4.7)

waypoints.name: M85
waypoints.instruction: M85 is about 1 degree east-northeast of 11 Comae Berenices

routes.notes: M85 lies midway between Denebola and Vindemiatrix but slightly north of the line. Northernmost member of the Virgo Cluster.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1), the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8) about 15 degrees to the east

waypoints.name: M84
waypoints.instruction: Aim halfway between Denebola and Vindemiatrix to find M84

waypoints.name: M86
waypoints.instruction: M86 sits just 17 arcminutes east of M84, both visible in the same eyepiece. M86 has a slightly more oval appearance

routes.source: BBC Sky at Night / Galactic Hunter
routes.notes: M86 is part of Markarian's Chain and often found together with M84 as a pair.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1) at the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8) in Virgo, about 15 degrees east

waypoints.name: M87
waypoints.instruction: M87 is just over halfway along the Denebola-Vindemiatrix line, about 1.5 degrees southeast of the M84/M86 pair. Giant elliptical galaxy appearing as a featureless fuzzy ball

routes.notes: Central giant of the Virgo Cluster, containing a famous supermassive black hole. M84/M86 lie 1.5 degrees to the northwest.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1) in Leo

waypoints.name: M84/M86 pair
waypoints.instruction: Navigate to the M84/M86 pair halfway between Denebola and Vindemiatrix

waypoints.name: M87
waypoints.instruction: Move 1.5 degrees southeast to M87

waypoints.name: M89
waypoints.instruction: Continue about 1 degree east to M89

waypoints.name: M90
waypoints.instruction: From M89, move 0.75 degrees north to M90

waypoints.name: M88
waypoints.instruction: From M90, move 1.5 degrees northwest to M88. It is a bright, elongated spiral galaxy

routes.notes: M88 is 50 arcminutes west of M91, so both fit in the same low-to-medium power field.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8) in Virgo

waypoints.name: Rho Virginis
waypoints.instruction: Move 5 degrees west and slightly south to Rho Virginis (mag 4.9)

waypoints.name: M60
waypoints.instruction: From Rho Virginis, move 1.5 degrees north to M60

waypoints.name: M59
waypoints.instruction: M59 is 0.4 degrees west of M60

waypoints.name: M58
waypoints.instruction: Continue 1 degree west to M58

waypoints.name: M89
waypoints.instruction: From M58, move slightly west and 1 degree north to M89, a nearly perfectly circular elliptical galaxy

routes.notes: M89 is nearly perfectly spherical -- the most circular elliptical galaxy known. Central hub for reaching M90, M87, and M58.

waypoints.name: Vindemiatrix (Epsilon Virginis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vindemiatrix (mag 2.8) in Virgo

waypoints.name: M84/M86
waypoints.instruction: Navigate to the M84/M86 pair halfway between Denebola and Vindemiatrix

waypoints.name: M87
waypoints.instruction: Move 1.5 degrees southeast to M87

waypoints.name: M89
waypoints.instruction: Move about 1 degree east to M89

waypoints.name: M90
waypoints.instruction: M90 is 0.75 degrees north of M89. One of the largest spirals in the Virgo Cluster, visible in the same field as M89

routes.notes: M90 is one of the few galaxies approaching us (blueshifted). It forms a nice pair with M89 in the same low-power field.

targetName: Barred Spiral Galaxy in Coma Berenices

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1) and navigate to the Virgo Cluster core

waypoints.name: M89
waypoints.instruction: Locate M89 in the Virgo Cluster core (see M89 route)

waypoints.name: M90
waypoints.instruction: Move 0.75 degrees north to M90

waypoints.name: M91
waypoints.instruction: From M90, move about 1 degree north-northwest. M91 is one of the faintest Messier objects and very hard to see; M88 is 50 arcminutes to its west

routes.notes: One of the faintest Messier objects. Very difficult in small apertures. Hopeless from light-polluted sites.

waypoints.name: Pi Herculis
waypoints.instruction: Start at Pi Herculis (mag 3.2), at the northern end of the Keystone asterism in Hercules

waypoints.name: Iota Herculis
waypoints.instruction: Identify Iota Herculis (mag 3.8), outside the Keystone to the northeast

waypoints.name: M92
waypoints.instruction: M92 lies about one-third of the way from Pi to Iota Herculis. It's a magnificent globular cluster at mag 6.4, just on the edge of naked-eye visibility under very dark skies

routes.notes: Often overshadowed by M13 nearby but M92 is a superb globular in its own right. One of the oldest known globular clusters.

waypoints.name: Sirius (Alpha Canis Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky

waypoints.name: Omicron 1 & 2 Canis Majoris
waypoints.instruction: Move about a fist-width (10 degrees) southeast to the north-south pairing of Omicron 1 and 2 CMa

waypoints.name: Xi Puppis
waypoints.instruction: Continue another 10 degrees in the same direction to reach Xi Puppis (mag 3.3)

waypoints.name: M93
waypoints.instruction: M93 lies 1.5 degrees northwest of Xi Puppis. It is a bright open cluster (mag 6.0) resembling a butterfly, visible in binoculars

routes.notes: Bright and easy in any optics. Best viewed in winter months when Puppis is visible.

waypoints.name: Cor Caroli (Alpha CVn)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Cor Caroli (mag 2.9), the brightest star in Canes Venatici

waypoints.name: Chara (Beta CVn)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Chara (mag 4.2), about 5 degrees northwest of Cor Caroli

waypoints.name: M94
waypoints.instruction: M94 forms a flat equilateral triangle with Cor Caroli and Chara, positioned to the north. It is about 3 degrees east and slightly south of Chara, or about 2 finger-widths north of Cor Caroli

routes.notes: M94 has a very bright, compact core. Through binoculars it appears bright and compact but not point-shaped.

waypoints.name: Regulus (Alpha Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Regulus (mag 1.4), the heart of Leo

waypoints.name: 52 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Move about halfway along the Lion's belly between Regulus and Chertan to find 52 Leonis (mag 5.5)

waypoints.name: 53 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: 53 Leonis (mag 5.3) appears just south of 52 Leonis in the same field

waypoints.name: M96
waypoints.instruction: M96 lies centered between 52 and 53 Leonis

waypoints.name: M95
waypoints.instruction: M95 is about 40 arcminutes west of M96. It is a barred spiral galaxy

routes.notes: M95, M96, and M105 form a trio. All three challenge binoculars even under dark, clear skies.

waypoints.name: Regulus (Alpha Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Regulus (mag 1.4), the heart of Leo

waypoints.name: 52 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Move halfway between Regulus and Chertan to find 52 Leonis (mag 5.5)

waypoints.name: 53 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Place 52 Leonis at the top of the field; 53 Leonis appears at the bottom

waypoints.name: M96
waypoints.instruction: M96 is centered in the field between 52 and 53 Leonis. It is the brightest of the trio, an intermediate spiral galaxy

routes.notes: M96 is the eponymous member of the M96 galaxy group, which also includes M95 and M105.

waypoints.name: Merak (Beta Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Merak (mag 2.3), the southwest corner of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: M97
waypoints.instruction: Move about 2.5 degrees east-southeast of Merak, just south of the line connecting Merak and Phecda. The Owl Nebula appears as a faint ball of light. M108 lies about 48 arcminutes northwest, and both fit in one low-power field

routes.notes: The 'owl eyes' are visible in 10-inch and larger telescopes. M108 is in the same field, making a popular pairing.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1) at the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: 6 Comae Berenices
waypoints.instruction: Move about 7 degrees east to find 6 Comae Berenices, part of a distinctive T-shaped asterism of five stars

waypoints.name: M98
waypoints.instruction: M98 lies half a degree due west of 6 Comae Berenices. It is a dim, elongated spiral galaxy and one of the most difficult galaxies in the Virgo Cluster

routes.notes: M98, M99, and M100 all cluster around 6 Comae Berenices, each within 0.5 degrees. Found by Mechain on the same night in 1781.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1), the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: 6 Comae Berenices
waypoints.instruction: Move about 7 degrees east to 6 Comae Berenices in the T-shaped asterism

waypoints.name: M99
waypoints.instruction: M99 is just under 1 degree southeast of 6 Comae Berenices. It is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy

routes.notes: M99 is one of the brighter spirals in the Virgo Cluster, discovered by Mechain on the same night as M98.

waypoints.name: Denebola (Beta Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Denebola (mag 2.1) at the tail of Leo

waypoints.name: 6 Comae Berenices
waypoints.instruction: Move about 7 degrees east to 6 Comae Berenices

waypoints.name: M100
waypoints.instruction: From 6 Comae, proceed northeast through two mag-6 stars, each about 0.5 degrees apart, continuing about 1 degree total. M100 is the brightest of the three galaxies near 6 Comae, a grand-design spiral

routes.notes: M100 is one of the brightest and largest spirals in the Virgo Cluster. Best seen in spring.

waypoints.name: Mizar (Zeta Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Mizar (mag 2.2), the second star from the end of the Big Dipper's handle, with its famous companion Alcor

waypoints.name: Alkaid (Eta Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Identify Alkaid (mag 1.9) at the tip of the handle

waypoints.name: M101
waypoints.instruction: Form an equilateral triangle on the scoop-side of the handle using Mizar and Alkaid as the base. M101 sits at the apex of this triangle, about 5.5 degrees northeast of Mizar. It is a large, face-on spiral with low surface brightness

routes.notes: Alternative: from Mizar-Alcor, follow a chain of 5th-6th mag stars (81, 83, 84, 86 UMa) to M101. Requires dark, transparent skies.

waypoints.name: Iota Draconis (Edasich)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Edasich (mag 3.3), a red giant star in the southern part of Draco

waypoints.name: M102
waypoints.instruction: Move about 4 degrees southwest of Edasich, in the direction of Alkaid (Eta Ursae Majoris) at the tip of the Big Dipper's handle. M102 appears as a considerably bright elliptical or spindle-shaped nebulous object with a brighter core

routes.notes: M102's identity is debated; commonly identified as NGC 5866. A lenticular galaxy with a visible dust lane in larger apertures.

waypoints.name: Ruchbah (Delta Cassiopeiae)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Ruchbah (mag 2.7), one of the bright stars forming Cassiopeia's W asterism

waypoints.name: M103
waypoints.instruction: M103 is about 1 degree east of Ruchbah, along the line toward Epsilon Cassiopeiae. It appears as a diamond-shaped patch in binoculars, resolving into about 40 stars in a small telescope

routes.notes: Very easy to locate from the unmistakable Cassiopeia W. Dominated by the foreground multiple star Struve 131.

waypoints.name: Delta Corvi (Algorab)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Delta Corvi (mag 2.9), in the trapezoid of Corvus the Crow

waypoints.name: Triangular star group
waypoints.instruction: Move about 3 degrees north to a triangular arrangement of 5th-6th magnitude stars

waypoints.name: Close pair of 6th-mag stars
waypoints.instruction: Follow the line of the triangle about 1 degree further to a close pair of mid-6th magnitude stars

waypoints.name: M104
waypoints.instruction: Move 1 degree east-northeast from this pair to reach M104. The Sombrero Galaxy appears as an elongated glow; larger telescopes reveal its famous dark dust lane

routes.notes: Also findable roughly halfway between Spica and the star Epsilon Corvi. The dust lane is visible in 4-inch telescopes under good conditions.

waypoints.name: Regulus (Alpha Leonis)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Regulus (mag 1.4) in Leo

waypoints.name: 52 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: Move halfway between Regulus and Chertan to find 52 Leonis (mag 5.5)

waypoints.name: 53 Leonis
waypoints.instruction: 53 Leonis is just south of 52 Leonis in the same field

waypoints.name: M105
waypoints.instruction: M105 is the northernmost galaxy of the Leo trio, about 50 arcminutes north of M96. It is an elliptical galaxy, the brightest member of the M96 group

routes.notes: M105 forms a compact group with NGC 3384 and NGC 3389, all three visible in the same eyepiece field.

waypoints.name: Phecda (Gamma Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Phecda (mag 2.4), the lower-left star of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: M106
waypoints.instruction: Move about 7.5 degrees southeast from Phecda, roughly halfway toward Cor Caroli. M106 is a bright spiral galaxy visible in binoculars under dark conditions

routes.notes: Alternative: extend a line from Dubhe through Phecda; M106 lies at roughly the same distance beyond. One of the nearest Seyfert galaxies.

waypoints.name: Zeta Ophiuchi
waypoints.instruction: Start at Zeta Ophiuchi (mag 2.6), located about 16 degrees north of Antares

waypoints.name: M107
waypoints.instruction: Move about 2.75 degrees south-southwest of Zeta Ophiuchi. M107 is a loose globular cluster of 8th magnitude, a difficult binocular object but easier in small telescopes

routes.notes: Best in late spring and early summer. Lies between Beta Scorpii and Beta Ophiuchi.

waypoints.name: Merak (Beta Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Merak (mag 2.3), the southwest corner of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: M108
waypoints.instruction: Move 1.5 degrees southeast of Merak along the Merak-Phecda line, then 0.3 degrees south-southwest. M108 is an edge-on barred spiral appearing as a streaky spindle with mottled texture. The Owl Nebula (M97) lies about 48 arcminutes to its southeast

routes.source: BBC Sky at Night / Optical Mechanics
routes.notes: M108 and M97 (Owl Nebula) make a popular pairing in the same low-power field.

targetName: Barred Spiral Galaxy in Ursa Major

waypoints.name: Phecda (Gamma Ursae Majoris)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Phecda (mag 2.4), the lower-left star of the Big Dipper's bowl

waypoints.name: M109
waypoints.instruction: M109 is less than 1 degree southeast of Phecda. Because Phecda is bright, glare can hinder detection -- use low power first then increase magnification to improve contrast

routes.notes: One of the closest Messier galaxies to a bright naked-eye star. A barred spiral galaxy with a distinctive central bar.

waypoints.name: Alpheratz
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alpheratz (mag 2.1), the top-left star of the Great Square of Pegasus, which is also Alpha Andromedae

waypoints.name: Mirach (Beta Andromedae)
waypoints.instruction: Hop two stars along the Andromeda chain to Mirach (mag 2.1)

waypoints.name: Mu Andromedae
waypoints.instruction: Turn 90 degrees north from Mirach and hop one step to the fainter star Mu Andromedae (mag 3.9)

waypoints.name: M31
waypoints.instruction: Continue the same distance beyond Mu Andromedae to find M31, the great Andromeda Galaxy

waypoints.name: M110
waypoints.instruction: M110 is a diffuse elliptical galaxy about 35 arcminutes northwest of M31's core. It is larger but fainter than M32 (which is on the opposite side), and best seen under darker skies with averted vision

routes.source: EarthSky / BBC Sky at Night / Turn Left at Orion
routes.notes: M110 is the last object in the Messier catalog. A satellite galaxy of M31, more diffuse and challenging than M32.

waypoints.name: Orion's Belt
waypoints.instruction: Start at the three stars of Orion's Belt (Alnitak, Alnilam, Mintaka), one of the most recognizable patterns in the sky

waypoints.name: Aldebaran
waypoints.instruction: Extend a line through the Belt toward the upper-right (northwest) for about 15 degrees. You will reach the bright orange star Aldebaran (mag 0.9), the eye of Taurus the Bull

waypoints.name: Hyades
waypoints.instruction: Aldebaran sits at the tip of a prominent V-shaped pattern of stars spanning about 5.5 degrees. This is the Hyades cluster. Although Aldebaran appears in the V, it is actually a foreground star not part of the cluster. Binoculars reveal up to 100 stars including Theta-1 and Theta-2 Tauri, Gamma, Delta, and Epsilon Tauri within the V

routes.source: EarthSky; BBC Sky at Night Magazine; Space.com
routes.notes: The nearest open cluster to Earth at 153 light-years. Best viewed with wide-field binoculars (7x35 or 7x50) with fields of 8-10 degrees. A naked-eye object, but binoculars transform it into a rich scatter of dozens of stars. Aldebaran, though visually in the V, is at 65 light-years and not a cluster member

waypoints.name: Altair (Alpha Aql)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Altair (mag 0.8), the brightest star in Aquila and the southern vertex of the Summer Triangle

waypoints.name: Tarazed (Gamma Aql)
waypoints.instruction: Look 2 degrees northwest of Altair for the orange star Tarazed (mag 2.7)

waypoints.name: Coathanger
waypoints.instruction: Continue northwest from Tarazed about 8 degrees further (one-third of the way toward Vega). Against a darker region of the Milky Way you will find 10 stars arranged unmistakably in a coat-hanger shape, with a straight bar of 6 stars and a hook of 4 stars

routes.source: EarthSky; BBC Sky at Night Magazine
routes.notes: Visible to the naked eye as an unresolved glow. Sweep slowly along the Altair-to-Vega line. Best seen in July-August from northern latitudes

waypoints.name: Albireo (Beta Cyg)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Albireo (mag 3.1) at the foot of the Northern Cross in Cygnus

waypoints.name: Alpha Vulpeculae
waypoints.instruction: Move about 6 degrees south-southwest to Alpha Vulpeculae (Anser, mag 4.4)

waypoints.name: Coathanger
waypoints.instruction: Continue the same distance beyond Alpha Vulpeculae in the same direction. The Coathanger will appear as a prominent line of stars with a hook hanging down

routes.notes: The Coathanger appears 'upside down' (hook pointing up) when Vulpecula is high overhead

waypoints.name: Betelgeuse (Alpha Ori)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Betelgeuse (mag 0.5), the red supergiant at Orion's upper-left shoulder

waypoints.name: Bellatrix (Gamma Ori)
waypoints.instruction: Note Bellatrix (mag 1.6) at Orion's upper-right shoulder, about 8 degrees west of Betelgeuse

waypoints.name: Meissa / Collinder 69
waypoints.instruction: Look 3 degrees north of the midpoint between Betelgeuse and Bellatrix for Meissa (Lambda Orionis, mag 3.4). This marks Orion's head. Surrounding Meissa is a triangular grouping of stars including Phi-1 and Phi-2 Orionis forming the core of Collinder 69. Binoculars reveal about 20 stars scattered across just over 1 degree

routes.source: Astronomy.com; British Astronomical Association; Houston Astronomical Society
routes.notes: Often overlooked but easy to find at Orion's head. The compact triangle of Meissa + Phi-1 + Phi-2 is visible to the naked eye. Binoculars show additional cluster members. Meissa illuminates a vast ring of gas 150 light-years across (Sh2-264), though the ring itself is a photographic target only

waypoints.name: Deneb (Alpha Cyg)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Deneb (mag 1.25), the tail of the swan at the top of the Northern Cross

waypoints.name: Sadr (Gamma Cyg)
waypoints.instruction: Move about 7 degrees south-southwest to Sadr (mag 2.2), the central star of the Northern Cross where the beam and crossbar intersect

waypoints.name: Eta Cygni
waypoints.instruction: Continue about 7 degrees further south along the swan's neck to Eta Cygni (mag 3.9)

waypoints.name: Albireo (Beta Cyg)
waypoints.instruction: Continue another 6 degrees south-southwest to reach Albireo (mag 3.1) at the head of the swan. In binoculars or any small telescope, it splits into a vivid gold and blue pair separated by 34 arcseconds

routes.notes: Follow the beam of the Northern Cross from Deneb down through Sadr to its base. In 10x50 binoculars mounted on a tripod the two components may just be split; a small telescope easily shows the famous gold-and-sapphire color contrast

waypoints.name: Vega (Alpha Lyr)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Vega (mag 0.0), the brilliant blue-white star and the fifth-brightest star in the entire sky

waypoints.name: Epsilon Lyrae (Double-Double)
waypoints.instruction: Look just 1.6 degrees northeast of Vega. Epsilon Lyrae is the nearest bright companion star to Vega and fits easily in the same binocular field. In binoculars, what appears as one star to the naked eye resolves into two components (Epsilon-1 and Epsilon-2), separated by about 3.5 arcminutes. Each of these is itself a close double, but resolving the sub-pairs requires a telescope

routes.source: EarthSky; Almanac.com; BBC Sky at Night Magazine
routes.notes: One of the easiest star-hops in the sky since it is less than 2 degrees from Vega. The wide pair splits easily in any binoculars. The four-star nature (double-double) requires ~100x magnification in a telescope

waypoints.name: Alkaid (Eta UMa)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Alkaid (mag 1.9), the star at the very tip of the Big Dipper's handle

waypoints.name: Mizar & Alcor
waypoints.instruction: Move one star inward along the handle (about 5 degrees) to the second star from the end. This is Mizar (mag 2.3). Look closely with your naked eye or binoculars and you will see Alcor (mag 4.0) sitting just 12 arcminutes away (about one-third of the Moon's diameter). Binoculars also reveal that Mizar itself has a fainter companion (Mizar B) at about 14 arcseconds separation

routes.source: EarthSky; Sky & Telescope; BBC Sky at Night Magazine
routes.notes: The ancient eye test: try to see Alcor with unaided vision first. Known as the Horse and Rider. In binoculars the pair is obvious. Mizar was the first double star discovered telescopically (1617)

waypoints.name: Rasalhague (Alpha Oph)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Rasalhague (mag 2.1), the brightest star in Ophiuchus, located at the top (head) of the constellation

waypoints.name: Cebalrai (Beta Oph)
waypoints.instruction: Move about 8 degrees south to Cebalrai (mag 2.8), a noticeable star in the upper body of Ophiuchus

waypoints.name: IC 4665
waypoints.instruction: IC 4665 lies just 1 degree north-northeast of Cebalrai, close enough to appear in the same binocular field. You will see approximately 25 stars spread over about 1 square degree. If southwest is 'up' in your field of view, the inner stars spell out the letters 'HI'

routes.notes: One of the largest open clusters for binoculars (41 arcmin). Also called the Summer Beehive. At magnitude 4.2 it is barely visible to the naked eye. Best observed in summer months when Ophiuchus is high. The 'HI' pattern in the inner stars is a fun feature

waypoints.name: Caph (Beta Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Caph (mag 2.3), the rightmost (westernmost) star of Cassiopeia's W

waypoints.name: Segin (Epsilon Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Move to Segin (mag 3.4), the leftmost (easternmost) star of Cassiopeia's W, about 13 degrees away

waypoints.name: Kemble's Cascade
waypoints.instruction: Extend the Caph-to-Segin line the same distance beyond Segin toward the southeast into Camelopardalis. You will encounter a stunning chain of 20+ stars stretching over 2.5 degrees, ending in the fuzzy patch of open cluster NGC 1502

routes.source: BBC Sky at Night Magazine; Wikipedia
routes.notes: Stars range from 5th to 9th magnitude. More than a dozen are visible in 10x50 binoculars. Best seen on early evenings during November-December. The chain has a striking waterfall appearance

waypoints.name: Betelgeuse (Alpha Ori)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Betelgeuse (mag 0.5), the bright red supergiant marking Orion's shoulder

waypoints.name: Triangle in Monoceros
waypoints.instruction: Look about 10 degrees east of Betelgeuse for a triangle of three 4th-magnitude stars forming the head of Monoceros the Unicorn (13 Mon, Epsilon Mon, and nearby stars)

waypoints.name: Rosette Cluster (NGC 2244)
waypoints.instruction: The Rosette Cluster (mag 4.8) lies within this triangle. In binoculars you will see an obvious cluster of stars. The Rosette Nebula (NGC 2237) surrounds the cluster but is extremely faint and requires dark skies and possibly an OIII filter to detect visually

routes.source: Constellation Guide; Astronomy Now; Galactic Hunter
routes.notes: The cluster at mag 4.8 is easy; the surrounding nebula at mag 9 is challenging. The nebula spans over twice the diameter of the full Moon. Best viewed in February when Monoceros is high. Also located roughly halfway between Betelgeuse and Procyon, slightly south of the midpoint line

waypoints.name: Betelgeuse (Alpha Ori)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Betelgeuse (mag 0.5), the red supergiant at Orion's shoulder

waypoints.name: 15 Monocerotis / NGC 2264
waypoints.instruction: Move about 13 degrees east and 3 degrees north from Betelgeuse into Monoceros. The Christmas Tree Cluster is centered on the star 15 Monocerotis (mag 4.6), which forms the 'trunk' of the tree. In binoculars the inverted tree shape is evident, with about 20 stars spanning 20 arcminutes. The associated Cone Nebula requires photography to see

routes.notes: At magnitude 3.9, the cluster is easy in binoculars. The tree appears inverted (point down) in a standard view. Located roughly 4 degrees north of the Rosette Cluster, so both can be visited in one binocular session through Monoceros

waypoints.name: Ruchbah (Delta Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Ruchbah (mag 2.7), the fourth star of Cassiopeia's W (counting from the right/west)

waypoints.name: NGC 457 (ET Cluster)
waypoints.instruction: Move 2 degrees south-southeast of Ruchbah. You will find the cluster adjacent to the bright star Phi Cassiopeiae (mag 5.0). Phi Cas and the nearby star HD 7902 form the two bright 'eyes' of the owl/ET figure. The cluster's fainter members spread out to form arms and a body below the eyes

routes.notes: Very easy to find thanks to its proximity to the W of Cassiopeia. Binoculars show the bright 'eyes' (Phi Cas) and a fuzzy glow; a small telescope reveals the full stick-figure shape. Also known as Caldwell 13

targetName: NGC 6231 (Northern Jewel Box / Table of Scorpius)

waypoints.name: Shaula (Lambda Sco)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Shaula (mag 1.6) at the stinger of Scorpius's tail, one of the brightest stars in the constellation

waypoints.name: Zeta Scorpii pair
waypoints.instruction: Move about 6 degrees northwest along the tail to the Zeta Scorpii pair (Zeta-1 at mag 4.7 and Zeta-2 at mag 3.6), a colorful optical double in binoculars

waypoints.name: NGC 6231
waypoints.instruction: NGC 6231 lies less than 1 degree north of the Zeta Scorpii pair. It appears as a brilliant, compact cluster with numerous stars resolvable in binoculars. At magnitude 2.6, it is visible to the naked eye and spectacular in any binoculars

routes.source: Constellation Guide; Cloudy Nights; NASA Caldwell Catalog
