routes.notes: Also called the Table of Scorpius cluster and Caldwell 76. Spectacular from southern latitudes. The surrounding region includes Trumpler 24 and Collinder 316 forming a 'false comet' shape in binoculars. Best viewed in July-August when Scorpius is well-placed

waypoints.name: Deneb (Alpha Cyg)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Deneb (mag 1.25), the tail star of Cygnus and the brightest star at the top of the Northern Cross

waypoints.name: North America Nebula
waypoints.instruction: Move 3 degrees east of Deneb. Under dark skies the nebula appears as a large foggy patch roughly twice the diameter of the full Moon. Xi Cygni (mag 3.7) marks its southeastern edge. An OIII or UHC filter held in front of the eyepiece greatly improves contrast

routes.notes: Very low surface brightness; requires dark skies for visual detection. Wide-field binoculars (7x50 or similar) with a 3+ degree field work best. The nebula appears grey; an OIII filter helps enormously

waypoints.name: Almach (Gamma And)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Almach (mag 2.1), the third-brightest star in Andromeda, a beautiful gold-and-blue double star at the eastern end of the Andromeda chain

waypoints.name: NGC 752
waypoints.instruction: Move 5 degrees south and slightly west of Almach. The cluster is also located roughly halfway between Almach and Beta Trianguli. NGC 752 appears as a large scattered glow in binoculars, with the brightest dozen stars individually resolvable in 10x50 binoculars. The cluster spans about 50 arcminutes, comparable to the full Moon

routes.notes: At magnitude 5.7, easily visible in binoculars. A large, loose cluster ideal for low-power observation. Can also be found by using Gamma and Beta Trianguli as pointer stars, extending a line northward about 1.5x their separation. Discovered by Caroline Herschel in 1783

waypoints.name: Caph (Beta Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Caph (mag 2.3), the rightmost (westernmost) star of Cassiopeia's W

waypoints.name: Schedar (Alpha Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Note Schedar (mag 2.2), the next star along the W from Caph, about 5 degrees to the east

waypoints.name: NGC 7789 (Caroline's Rose)
waypoints.instruction: Form a right triangle outside the W using Caph and Schedar as the two base points. NGC 7789 lies at the third vertex, about 3 degrees southwest of Caph. Through binoculars it appears as a misty half-Moon-sized patch between the 5th-magnitude stars Sigma and Rho Cassiopeiae. Binoculars reveal a haze of unresolved stars; a telescope shows the rose petal pattern of curving star lanes

routes.notes: Discovered by Caroline Herschel in 1783. At magnitude 6.7 it is visible in binoculars as a fuzzy glow under dark skies but needs a telescope to see the distinctive rose-petal pattern of interleaving star chains. Contains roughly 1000 stars at a distance of 7600 light-years

waypoints.name: Mirfak (Alpha Per)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Mirfak (mag 1.8), the brightest star in Perseus

waypoints.name: Double Cluster
waypoints.instruction: Scan about 8 degrees northwest from Mirfak toward Cassiopeia. The Double Cluster lies roughly halfway between Mirfak and Gamma Cassiopeiae (the center star of the W). Two adjacent fuzzy patches resolve into twin star clusters in binoculars

routes.source: Freestarcharts.com; Phil Harrington, Touring the Universe Through Binoculars
routes.notes: Visible to the naked eye as a fuzzy patch between Perseus and Cassiopeia. Magnificent in any binoculars, filling the field with hundreds of stars

waypoints.name: Segin (Epsilon Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Segin, the westernmost star of Cassiopeia's W

waypoints.name: Miram (Eta Per)
waypoints.instruction: Hop about 7 degrees southeast to Miram (Eta Persei, mag 3.8), the northernmost bright star of Perseus

waypoints.name: Double Cluster
waypoints.instruction: The Double Cluster lies about 4.5 degrees northwest of Miram, roughly midway between Segin and Miram. Both clusters fit in the same binocular field of view

routes.notes: This route approaches from Cassiopeia's side. Also works well by extending a line from Gamma Cas through Ruchbah (Delta Cas) to the southeast

waypoints.name: Double Cluster (NGC 869/884)
waypoints.instruction: Start at the Double Cluster between Perseus and Cassiopeia (find it using any of the Double Cluster routes above)

waypoints.name: Stock 2
waypoints.instruction: From the Cassiopeia-side (northern edge) of the Double Cluster, follow a 2-degree curved chain of stars leading northward. Stock 2 appears as a roughly degree-wide scatter of 9th-10th magnitude stars forming a decapitated stick-figure flexing its muscles. In binoculars the Muscleman is spread over about 1 degree with its feet to the east in a bicep-flexing pose

routes.notes: Splendid in binoculars and rich-field telescopes; loses its grandeur in narrow telescope fields. Located at the Cassiopeia/Perseus border. The cluster appears to be hauling the Double Cluster on a leash of stars

waypoints.name: Gamma Cas (center of W)
waypoints.instruction: Start at Gamma Cassiopeiae (mag 2.5), the center star of Cassiopeia's W

waypoints.name: Ruchbah (Delta Cas)
waypoints.instruction: Move to Ruchbah (mag 2.7), the next star eastward in the W

waypoints.name: Stock 2
waypoints.instruction: Continue the line from Gamma Cas through Ruchbah and extend it about 5 degrees further to the southeast. Stock 2 appears as a broad scatter of stars about 1 degree across, just 2 degrees north of the Double Cluster

routes.source: BBC Sky at Night Magazine; BinocularSky.com
routes.notes: The same sweep that finds the Double Cluster passes near Stock 2, which lies just to the north
